Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a strict study routine. I'd mostly listen to lecture recordings while doing other things—commuting, cooking, or just lying in bed. I found that hearing information helped it stick better than reading, so I'd replay audio content multiple times. My study hours were pretty scattered throughout the week, maybe totaling around 14 hours, but it wasn't planned or anything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due. I'd have bursts of activity when deadlines approached, but otherwise I'd drift through the week without much structure. Sometimes I'd go days without touching course material, then suddenly spend a whole evening catching up. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for my grades somehow.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification would prompt me to log in. I wasn't one of those students who checked the LMS daily out of habit. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study offline later. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more often because earning points felt oddly satisfying.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do. The gamified elements helped a bit because I'd want to check my progress or see if I'd unlocked anything new. But calling it a 'routine' would be a stretch; it was more like sporadic visits.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded content were by far the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd download them and listen repeatedly until concepts clicked. The gamified tasks also helped because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks that felt less overwhelming than looking at the whole course at once.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums—I only posted once the entire semester. Reading through other people's posts felt time-consuming and I wasn't sure it added much to my understanding. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I could usually get what I needed from the lectures alone.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could directly connect it to doing well on exams or assignments. The gamified tasks were good because they gave immediate feedback and felt like mini-achievements. Anything that seemed like busywork or wouldn't clearly help my grade, I'd probably skip or rush through.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, honestly. Required ones had this weight of consequence attached to them. With optional stuff, I'd think 'well, if I don't do this, nothing bad happens,' so I rarely did them unless they seemed genuinely interesting or the gamification made them appealing.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded audio files and notes I'd made while listening. I'd also use my own handwritten notes and sometimes YouTube videos to clarify concepts. The LMS was more like a starting point—I'd grab what I needed and then study elsewhere.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures on repeat while walking around or doing chores, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also mentally review concepts before falling asleep or discuss ideas with friends over coffee. My best learning often happened away from any screen or trackable system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download lecture recordings once and then listen to them five or six times over the following weeks. The LMS would only show one access, but that single download represented hours of engagement. Same with PDFs—I'd print them or save them to my tablet and study from there.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with anything academic. I'd feel this paralysis where logging in felt overwhelming even though I knew I should. My resilience isn't great, so when things got tough, I'd withdraw rather than push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars or felt fatigued, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, and sometimes I did, sometimes I didn't. Confusion about course material also made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about other things or just zoned out. I'd technically 'access' content but couldn't tell you what it said five minutes later.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because social academic interaction makes me anxious. I knew participating could help my understanding, but the thought of putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt uncomfortable. I'd also avoid difficult readings until the last possible moment, hoping they'd somehow become less intimidating.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I'd drift aimlessly through the course. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become hyper-focused and get everything done in a concentrated burst. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines that kept me somewhat on track between major assignments.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though my response time to check it was slow—sometimes days. When I got positive feedback, it boosted my mood and made me more willing to engage. Negative or critical feedback would make me avoid the course for a bit while I processed it emotionally.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I needed more external structure to stay engaged. The gamified elements added some structure that helped, giving me small goals to work toward between major deadlines.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—earning points and completing challenges felt rewarding in a way that just 'doing coursework' didn't. What made it harder was the sheer number of courses I was taking—fifteen online courses is a lot to juggle. Sometimes I couldn't give this course the attention it deserved.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—exploring, downloading, figuring out what they need, but then doing the actual work offline or struggling to translate activity into completed assignments. Or maybe they're anxious and keep checking things without feeling confident enough to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. I barely attended live sessions and my LMS activity was inconsistent, but I still got a Distinction. I did most of my real learning through downloaded materials and audio content that I engaged with repeatedly offline. The system couldn't see my actual study process.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you stay. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They definitely can't capture offline study, mental processing, or discussions with friends that might be more valuable than any logged activity.
Q23: Being engaged means the material actually occupies space in your mind—you think about it outside of study sessions, you make connections to other things, you care about understanding it. It's not about how many times you log in or how long you watch videos. It's about whether the learning is actually happening inside you.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I listened to downloaded lectures while going about my day, or how concepts would suddenly click while I was doing something completely unrelated. It wouldn't capture my anxiety about discussion posts or why I avoided certain activities. My engagement was real, just mostly invisible to the system.
